abstract.para: The term &clvm; indicates that LVM is being used in a cluster environment. When managing shared storage on a cluster, every node must be informed about changes to the storage subsystem. Logical Volume Manager (LVM) supports transparent management of volume groups across the whole cluster. Volume groups shared among multiple nodes can be managed using the same commands as local storage.

important.para: From &sle; 15 onward, the &ha; extension uses lvmlockd instead of clvmd.

listitem.para: LVM provides a virtual pool of disk space and enables flexible distribution of one logical volume over several disks.

Volume groups (VGs) and logical volumes (LVs) are basic concepts of LVM. A volume group is a storage pool of multiple physical disks. A logical volume belongs to a volume group, and can be seen as an elastic volume on which you can create a file system.

In a cluster environment, VGs consist of shared storage and can be used either by multiple nodes concurrently, or by one node at a time with the ability to migrate to other nodes. To protect the LVM metadata on shared storage, the cluster uses the resource agent LVM-activate to manage the activation of LVs in a particular VG. LV activation has two modes:

Exclusive mode should be used for local file systems like ext4. In this mode, LVs can only be activated on one node at a time. Exclusive access to the LV is managed either by &dlm; and lvmlockd or by using the system_id.
Shared mode should be used for cluster file systems like &gfs;. In this mode, LVs can be activated concurrently on multiple nodes. Locking is managed by &dlm; and lvmlockd.

important.para: lvmlockd with sanlock is not officially supported.

listitem.para: &dlm; coordinates cluster-wide locking. This is only required when using lvmlockd.
A shared storage device is available, such as Fibre Channel, FCoE, SCSI, &iscsi; SAN, or &nvmeof;, for example. Volume groups must have at least one disk, but typically have multiple disks.
Make sure the following packages are installed: lvm2 and lvm2-lockd.
From &sle; 15 onward, the &ha; extension uses lvmlockd instead of clvmd. Make sure the clvmd daemon is not running, otherwise lvmlockd will fail to start.

sect1.title: Configuring &clvm; with lvmlockd
sect1.para: You can configure &clvm; in one of the following ways, depending on your storage and cluster setup:

listitem.para: . Use this mode for cluster file systems like &gfs;. This is useful for active/active clusters where the VG is active on all nodes at once.
. Use this mode for local file systems like ext4. This is useful for active/passive clusters where only one node at a time has the VG active.

sect2.title: Configuring &clvm; in shared mode
sect2.para: Perform the following steps on one node to configure a shared VG for an active/active cluster:

step.para: Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Check the current configuration of the cluster resources:
If you have already configured a DLM resource (and a corresponding base group and base clone), continue with .
Otherwise, configure a DLM resource and a corresponding base group and base clone as described in .
Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Run the following command to see the usage of this resource:
Configure an lvmlockd resource as follows:
step.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure primitive lvmlockd lvmlockd \ op start timeout="90" \ op stop timeout="100" \ op monitor interval="30" timeout="90"

step.para: To ensure the lvmlockd resource is started on every node, add the primitive resource to the base group for storage you have created in :
Review your changes:
Check the status of the resources:

procedure.title: Creating a shared VG and LV

step.para: Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Assuming you already have two shared disks, create a shared VG with them:
Create an LV and do not activate it initially:
Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Run the following command to see the usage of this resource:
Configure a resource to manage the activation of your VG:
step.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure primitive vg1 LVM-activate \ params vgname=vg1 vg_access_mode=lvmlockd activation_mode=shared \ op start timeout=90s interval=0 \ op stop timeout=90s interval=0 \ op monitor interval=30s timeout=90s

step.para: Make sure the VG can only be activated on nodes where the DLM and lvmlockd resources are already running:

listitem.para: Because this VG is active on multiple nodes, you can add it to the cloned g-storage group, which already has internal colocation and order constraints:
Do not add multiple VGs to the group, because this creates a dependency between the VGs. For multiple VGs, clone the resources and add constraints to the clones:
listitem.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure clone cl-vg1 vg1 meta interleave=true &prompt.root;crm configure clone cl-vg2 vg2 meta interleave=true &prompt.root;crm configure colocation col-vg-with-dlm inf: ( cl-vg1 cl-vg2 ) cl-storage &prompt.root;crm configure order o-dlm-before-vg Mandatory: cl-storage ( cl-vg1 cl-vg2 )

step.para: Check the status of the resources:

sect2.title: Configuring &clvm; in exclusive mode
sect2.para: Perform the following steps on the active node to configure a local VG for an active/passive cluster:

step.para: Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Check the current configuration of the cluster resources:
If you have already configured a DLM resource (and a corresponding base group and base clone), continue with .
Otherwise, configure a DLM resource and a corresponding base group and base clone as described in .
Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Run the following command to see the usage of this resource:
Configure an lvmlockd resource as follows:
step.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure primitive lvmlockd lvmlockd \ op start timeout="90" \ op stop timeout="100" \ op monitor interval="30" timeout="90"

step.para: To ensure the lvmlockd resource is started on every node, add the primitive resource to the base group for storage you have created in :
Review your changes:
Check the status of the resources:

procedure.title: Creating a local VG and LV on shared storage

step.para: Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Assuming you already have two shared disks, create a local VG with them:
Create an LV and do not activate it initially:
Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Run the following command to see the usage of this resource:
Configure a resource to manage the activation of your VG:
step.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure primitive vg1 LVM-activate \ params vgname=vg1 vg_access_mode=lvmlockd \ op start timeout=90s interval=0 \ op stop timeout=90s interval=0 \ op monitor interval=30s timeout=90s
step.para: Exclusive activation mode is the default setting, so you don't need to specify it.
Make sure the VG can only be activated on nodes where the &dlm; and lvmlockd resources are already running:

listitem.para: Because this VG is only active on a single node, do not add it to the cloned g-storage group. Instead, add constraints directly to the resource:
listitem.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure colocation col-vg-with-dlm inf: vg1 cl-storage &prompt.root;crm configure order o-dlm-before-vg Mandatory: cl-storage vg1

listitem.para: For multiple VGs, you can add constraints to multiple resources at once:
listitem.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure colocation col-vg-with-dlm inf: ( vg1 vg2 ) cl-storage &prompt.root;crm configure order o-dlm-before-vg Mandatory: cl-storage ( vg1 vg2 )

step.para: Check the status of the resources:

sect1.title: Configuring LVM with system_id
sect1.para: Perform the following steps on the active node to configure a local VG for an active/passive cluster:

step.para: Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Open the /etc/lvm/lvm.conf file.
Make sure the use_lvmlockd line is commented out or set to 0.
Uncomment the system_id_source line and change the value to uname:

This means that the VG gets its system_id from the host name (uname -n) of whichever node the VG is currently active on. If the VG moves to another node, the system_id changes to the host name of the new node.
Save and exit the file.
Copy the updated configuration to all nodes:

procedure.title: Creating a local VG and LV on shared storage

step.para: Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Assuming you already have two shared disks, create a local VG with them:
Create an LV and do not activate it initially:
Reboot the other cluster nodes to refresh their LVM metadata.

tip.title: Refreshing LVM metadata without rebooting the nodes
tip.para: TODO pvscan --cache: Rescans the Physical Volumes.
TODO vgscan --cache: Rescans the Volume Groups.
TODO lvscan: Refreshes the status of the Logical Volumes.

step.para: Start a shell and log in as &rootuser;.
Run the following command to see the usage of this resource:
Configure a resource to manage the activation of your VG:
step.screen: &prompt.root;crm configure primitive vg1 LVM-activate \ params vgname=vg1 vg_access_mode=system_id \ op start timeout=90s interval=0 \ op stop timeout=90s interval=0 \ op monitor interval=30s timeout=90s
step.para: Exclusive activation mode is the default setting, so you don't need to specify it.
Check the status of the resource:

sect1.title: Scenario: &clvm; with iSCSI on SANs
sect1.para: The following scenario uses two SAN boxes which export their iSCSI targets to several clients. The general idea is displayed in .

figure.title: Setup of a shared disk with &clvm;

textobject.phrase: This diagram shows an LV on top of a VG on top of a PV. The PV is then connected to two separate iSCSI instances from SAN 1 and SAN 2, respectively.

warning.title: Data loss
warning.para: The following procedures will destroy any data on your disks.

sect1.para: Configure only one SAN box first. Each SAN box needs to export its own iSCSI target. Proceed as follows:

step.para: Run &yast; and click Network Services iSCSI LIO Target to start the iSCSI Server module.
If you want to start the iSCSI target whenever your computer is booted, choose When Booting, otherwise choose Manually.
If you have a firewall running, enable Open Port in Firewall.
Switch to the Global tab. If you need authentication, enable incoming or outgoing authentication or both. In this example, we select No Authentication.
Add a new iSCSI target:
Switch to the Targets tab.
Click Add.
Enter a target name. The name needs to be formatted like this:
For more information about the format, refer to Section 3.2.6.3.1. Type "iqn." (iSCSI Qualified Name) at .
If you want a more descriptive name, you can change it if your identifier is unique for your different targets.
Click Add.
Enter the device name in Path and use a Scsiid.
Click Next twice.
Confirm the warning box with Yes.
Open the configuration file /etc/iscsi/iscsid.conf and change the parameter node.startup to automatic.

sect1.para: Now set up your iSCSI initiators as follows:

step.para: Run &yast; and click Network Services iSCSI Initiator .
If you want to start the iSCSI initiator whenever your computer is booted, choose When Booting, otherwise set Manually.
Change to the Discovery tab and click the Discovery button.
If you use authentication, insert the incoming and outgoing user name and password, otherwise activate No Authentication.
Select Next. The found connections are displayed in the list.
Proceed with Finish.
Open a shell, log in as &rootuser;.
Test if the iSCSI initiator has been started successfully:
Establish a session:
step.screen: &prompt.root;iscsiadm -m node -l -p &wwwip; -T iqn.2010-03.de.&wsI;:san1 Logging in to [iface: default, target: iqn.2010-03.de.&wsI;:san1, portal: &wwwip;,3260] Login to [iface: default, target: iqn.2010-03.de.&wsI;:san1, portal: &wwwip;,3260]: successful
step.para: See the device names with lsscsi:
Look for entries with IET in their third column. In this case, the devices are /dev/sdd and /dev/sde.
Open a &rootuser; shell on one of the nodes you have run the iSCSI initiator from .
Create the shared volume group on disks /dev/sdd and /dev/sde, using their stable device names (for example, in /dev/disk/by-id/):
Create logical volumes as needed:
Check the volume group with vgdisplay:
step.screen: --- Volume group --- VG Name testvg System ID Format lvm2 Metadata Areas 2 Metadata Sequence No 1 VG Access read/write VG Status resizable MAX LV 0 Cur LV 0 Open LV 0 Max PV 0 Cur PV 2 Act PV 2 VG Size 1016,00 MB PE Size 4,00 MB Total PE 254 Alloc PE / Size 0 / 0 Free PE / Size 254 / 1016,00 MB VG UUID UCyWw8-2jqV-enuT-KH4d-NXQI-JhH3-J24anD

step.para: Check the shared state of the volume group with the command vgs:
step.screen: &prompt.root;vgs VG #PV #LV #SN Attr VSize VFree vgshared 1 1 0 wz--ns 1016.00m 1016.00m
step.para: The Attr column shows the volume attributes. In this example, the volume group is writable (w), resizable (z), the allocation policy is normal (n), and it is a shared resource (s). See the man page of vgs for details.

sect1.para: After you have created the volumes and started your resources you should have new device names under /dev/testvg, for example /dev/testvg/lv1. This indicates the LV has been activated for use.

sect1.title: Configuring eligible LVM devices explicitly
sect1.para: When several devices seemingly share the same physical volume signature (as can be the case for multipath devices or DRBD), we recommend to explicitly configure the devices which LVM scans for PVs.
For example, if the command vgcreate uses the physical device instead of using the mirrored block device, DRBD will be confused. This may result in a split-brain condition for DRBD.
To deactivate a single device for LVM, do the following:

step.para: Edit the file /etc/lvm/lvm.conf and search for the line starting with filter.
The patterns there are handled as regular expressions. A leading a means to accept a device pattern to the scan, a leading r rejects the devices that follow the device pattern.
To remove a device named /dev/sdb1, add the following expression to the filter rule:
The complete filter line will look like the following:
A filter line that accepts DRBD and MPIO devices but rejects all other devices would look like this:
Write the configuration file and copy it to all cluster nodes.

sect1.title: Online migration from mirror LV to cluster MD
sect1.para: Starting with &productname; 15, cmirrord in &clvm; is deprecated. We highly recommend to migrate the mirror logical volumes in your cluster to cluster MD. Cluster MD stands for cluster multi-device and is a software-based RAID storage solution for a cluster.

sect2.title: Example setup before migration
sect2.para: Let us assume you have the following example setup:

listitem.para: You have a two-node cluster consisting of the nodes &node1; and &node2;.
A mirror logical volume named test-lv was created from a volume group named cluster-vg2.
The volume group cluster-vg2 is composed of the disks /dev/vdb and /dev/vdc.

sect2.screen: &prompt.root;lsblk NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT vda 253:0 0 40G 0 disk ├─vda1 253:1 0 4G 0 part [SWAP] └─vda2 253:2 0 36G 0 part / vdb 253:16 0 20G 0 disk ├─cluster--vg2-test--lv_mlog_mimage_0 254:0 0 4M 0 lvm │ └─cluster--vg2-test--lv_mlog 254:2 0 4M 0 lvm │ └─cluster--vg2-test--lv 254:5 0 12G 0 lvm └─cluster--vg2-test--lv_mimage_0 254:3 0 12G 0 lvm └─cluster--vg2-test--lv 254:5 0 12G 0 lvm vdc 253:32 0 20G 0 disk ├─cluster--vg2-test--lv_mlog_mimage_1 254:1 0 4M 0 lvm │ └─cluster--vg2-test--lv_mlog 254:2 0 4M 0 lvm │ └─cluster--vg2-test--lv 254:5 0 12G 0 lvm └─cluster--vg2-test--lv_mimage_1 254:4 0 12G 0 lvm └─cluster--vg2-test--lv 254:5 0 12G 0 lvm

important.title: Avoiding migration failures
important.para: Before you start the migration procedure, check the capacity and degree of utilization of your logical and physical volumes. If the logical volume uses 100% of the physical volume capacity, the migration might fail with an insufficient free space error on the target volume. How to prevent this migration failure depends on the options used for mirror log:

formalpara.title: Is the mirror log itself mirrored (mirrored option) and allocated on the same device as the mirror leg?
formalpara.para: (For example, this might be the case if you have created the logical volume for a cmirrord setup on &productname; 11 or 12 as described in the &admin; for those versions.)

listitem.para: By default, mdadm reserves a certain amount of space between the start of a device and the start of array data. During migration, you can check for the unused padding space and reduce it with the data-offset option as shown in and following.

The data-offset must leave enough space on the device for cluster MD to write its metadata to it. However, the offset must be small enough for the remaining capacity of the device to accommodate all physical volume extents of the migrated volume. Because the volume may have spanned the complete device minus the mirror log, the offset must be smaller than the size of the mirror log.
We recommend to set the data-offset to 128 kB. If no value is specified for the offset, its default value is 1 kB (1024 bytes).

formalpara.title: Is the mirror log written to a different device (disk option) or kept in memory (core option)?
formalpara.para: Before starting the migration, either enlarge the size of the physical volume or reduce the size of the logical volume (to free more space for the physical volume).

sect2.title: Migrating a mirror LV to cluster MD
sect2.para: The following procedure is based on . Adjust the instructions to match your setup and replace the names for the LVs, VGs, disks, and the cluster MD device accordingly.
The migration does not involve any downtime. The file system can still be mounted during the migration procedure.

step.para: On node &node1;, execute the following steps:
Convert the mirror logical volume test-lv to a linear logical volume:
Remove the physical volume /dev/vdc from the volume group cluster-vg2:
Remove this physical volume from LVM:
When you run lsblk now, you get:
step.screen: NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT vda 253:0 0 40G 0 disk ├─vda1 253:1 0 4G 0 part [SWAP] └─vda2 253:2 0 36G 0 part / vdb 253:16 0 20G 0 disk └─cluster--vg2-test--lv 254:5 0 12G 0 lvm vdc 253:32 0 20G 0 disk

step.para: Create a cluster MD device /dev/md0 with the disk /dev/vdc:
For details on why to use the data-offset option, see .
On node &node2;, assemble this MD device:
If your cluster consists of more than two nodes, execute this step on all remaining nodes in your cluster.
Back on node &node1;:
Initialize the MD device /dev/md0 as physical volume for use with LVM:
Add the MD device /dev/md0 to the volume group cluster-vg2:
Move the data from the disk /dev/vdb to the /dev/md0 device:
Remove the physical volume /dev/vdb from the volume group cluster-vg2:
Remove the label from the device so that LVM no longer recognizes it as physical volume:
Add /dev/vdb to the MD device /dev/md0:

sect2.title: Example setup after migration
sect2.para: When you run lsblk now, you get:
sect2.screen: NAME MAJ:MIN RM SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT vda 253:0 0 40G 0 disk ├─vda1 253:1 0 4G 0 part [SWAP] └─vda2 253:2 0 36G 0 part / vdb 253:16 0 20G 0 disk └─md0 9:0 0 20G 0 raid1 └─cluster--vg2-test--lv 254:5 0 12G 0 lvm vdc 253:32 0 20G 0 disk └─md0 9:0 0 20G 0 raid1 └─cluster--vg2-test--lv 254:5 0 12G 0 lvm
